rationale: This is a classic, simple, and acyclic example of the Wolff-Kishner reduction. It uses a simple dialkyl ketone, 3-pentanone, which is reduced to the corresponding alkane, n-pentane. The reagents, hydrazine (NN) and potassium tert-butoxide ([O-]C(C)(C)C.[K+]), are standard for this transformation. The structure is minimal and clearly demonstrates the conversion of a C=O group to a CH2 group without introducing any other reactive functional groups or complex ring systems.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a simple, acyclic dialkyl ketone to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It avoids arenes (which could trigger Friedel-Crafts or other aromatic chemistry), alpha,beta-unsaturated systems (to avoid confusion with conjugate reductions like the Michael addition), and other functional groups. The focus is purely on the C=O to CH2 conversion, which is the core of the Wolff-Kishner reduction, distinguishing it from acidic reductions like the Clemmensen reduction.
